summary: Gruber systematically dismantles Yukari Kane's New Yorker piece arguing that post-Jobs Apple is a 'haunted empire' repeating the mistakes of the late 1980s. He challenges her claim that celebrating the Mac's 30th anniversary signals decline, arguing it was actually a healthy departure from what Jobs would have done. He rebuts her specific examples — the Browett hire, Forstall's firing, Apple Maps, iOS 7's bugs — by showing each is either internally contradictory or ignores that Jobs made comparable mistakes. Gruber frames Apple's prospects along three axes: advertising/retail (strong), incremental improvements (very strong), and big-bet new products (unknown but promising). He argues iOS 7's aggressive overhaul is actually evidence Apple remains willing to take bold chances. His core conclusion is that we simply don't yet know whether Apple can deliver breakthrough products without Jobs, and that Kane's premature verdict is driven by a book-promotion narrative rather than evidence.
key_points: Kane's parallel between 1985 Apple and 2014 Apple is flawed because Jobs was forced out in 1985 by executives who wanted him gone, whereas today's leadership admired and learned from him
Kane contradicts herself by lamenting Forstall's firing in one sentence and calling Apple Maps — Forstall's responsibility — a fiasco in the next

Dismissing all post-iPad products as lacking innovation ignores dramatic improvements like the iPad Air, iPhone 5S camera, and MacBook Air battery life — and forgets that even the original iPad was mocked as 'just a big iPhone'
iOS 7 is actually the strongest evidence that Apple remains willing to take big risks, overhauling a wildly popular platform's entire visual design in under 10 months
The honest answer about Apple's ability to deliver breakthrough products without Jobs is simply 'we don't know' — and claiming otherwise in either direction is premature
money_quotes: My sense of the pulse within the company is more along the lines of caged-tiger frustration, a sort of collective We loved Steve and miss him, but fuck you if you think this was a one-man show.

If Apple ceases to be Apple, if it fades to mediocrity and eventual irrelevance, it should not be seen as proof of Jobs's indispensability so much as a failure on Jobs's part to create an organization, culture, and tradition that could outlast him.
Kane can argue that Forstall should not have been fired. She can argue that the launch of Apple Maps was a firing offense. But she can't have it both ways.
A cell phone camera you can use to shoot Super Bowl commercial-quality video.
Apple's initial entries in new product categories are often — if not always — decried by some critics as not innovative.
iOS 7 is the best sign so far that Apple remains willing to take chances.
The simple truth, regarding Apple's continuing ability to deliver breakthrough new products, is that we have to wait.

The argument that iOS 7 would have been less buggy under Jobs's leadership holds no water. The problem isn't that Apple's engineers aren't working hard enough, or suffering from poor management. The problem is that Apple tried to do too much too quickly.
key_insight: Judging post-Jobs Apple requires distinguishing between incremental improvements (where the evidence is strong) and breakthrough new products (where we simply don't have enough data yet), and Kane's narrative collapses because she conflates the two while cherry-picking contradictory evidence.
